edge_case.title: Battery Health Decline Discovered After Plan Purchase
edge_case.description: Customer purchased AppleCare+ more than 30 days after buying the iPhone. Upon submitting a battery health claim that the battery capacity has depleted more than 50%, Apple must determine coverage eligibility since the defect may be pre-existing.

service_fee.notes: Battery service does not require ADH service fee, but eligibility depends on policy rules about pre-existing defects.

policy_references.snippet: Apple will repair or replace if battery capacity has depleted 50% or more from original specifications.
Plan does not apply to pre-existing conditions if Plan purchased after the iPhone.

edge_case.reasoning: The key tension arises between section 3.1 which covers battery degradation and section 4.2(viii) excluding pre-existing conditions if Plan is purchased post iPhone purchase. Since the Plan was bought late, Apple must assess whether the battery defect arose during the Plan Term or was pre-existing. Additional data such as diagnostic logs, original purchase dates, and battery health trends may be requested to clarify. Since battery claims do not have a service fee, no fee applies here.

edge_case.title: Claim for malfunction after unauthorized third-party repair
edge_case.description: Customer submits a claim for hardware failure that occurred shortly after a third-party repair where the device was opened and non-Apple parts were installed.

service_fee.notes: Service fee does not apply as there is no accidental damage claim; however, eligibility for free repair or replacement is uncertain.

policy_references.snippet: The Plan does not apply to service (including upgrades and expansions) if such was performed by anyone who is not a representative of Apple or an Apple Authorized Service Provider.

The Plan does not apply to an iPhone with a serial number that has been altered, defaced or removed, or has been modified to alter its functionality or capability without the written permission of the manufacturer.
Apple will repair or exchange the Covered iPhone if a defect in materials and workmanship arises during the Plan term.

edge_case.reasoning: The failure closely follows an unauthorized repair involving opening the device and installing non-Apple parts, which the Plan explicitly excludes under Sections 4.2(ii)(d) and 4.2(iii). However, the timing suggests a potential manufacturing defect triggering the failure, as per Section 3.1. This tension creates ambiguity whether failure is due to unauthorized modification or an inherent defect, necessitating investigation of serial integrity, repair history, and detailed claim information to resolve coverage eligibility. No service fee applies as this is not an ADH claim, and since the claim may be denied, the counts toward ADH limit is 'no'.

edge_case.title: Firmware update bricking device post-coverage
edge_case.description: Customer’s iPhone 14 experienced a firmware update failure that rendered the device inoperable three months after the AppleCare+ Plan expired. The malfunction appears hardware-related but occurred immediately after attempted iOS update.

service_fee.notes: Plan term ended; hardware failure timing causes coverage ambiguity.

policy_references.snippet: Coverage ends twenty-four (24) months from the date of retail purchase.
Hardware service covers defects during the Plan Term.
Plan excludes issues that could be resolved by upgrading software.

edge_case.reasoning: The device failure happened outside the Plan Term (section 2), so coverage is not explicitly applicable. However, the hardware failure coinciding with a software update raises uncertainty whether this was a latent manufacturing defect covered under warranty. Section 3.1 limits coverage to the Plan Term, while 4.3(ii) excludes software upgrade issues, necessitating verification if failure is software or hardware origin. Apple would request detailed diagnostics, proof of Plan expiration date, and iOS update logs to adjudicate under exclusion or possible manufacturer warranty.

edge_case.title: Battery Capacity Depletes Weeks After Plan Expiry
edge_case.description: Customer’s iPhone battery capacity drops below 50% just after the 24-month AppleCare+ plan term ended, raising a question about eligibility for battery service coverage.

service_fee.notes: Battery replacement under hardware service is free if within term, but here the failure occurred after term expiry.

policy_references.snippet: Coverage ends twenty-four (24) months from the date of retail purchase of your original iPhone (‘Plan Term’).
Apple covers battery service if battery capacity has depleted 50% or more ‘during the Plan Term’.
Apple limits liability to original plan price and does not guarantee uninterrupted operation beyond the plan term.

edge_case.reasoning: The claim lies precisely after plan expiration, triggering tension between the timing requirement in section 3.1 and the end of coverage in section 2. Apple’s liability limitation in section 8 also underscores no obligation to cover defects arising after term. Customer inputs such as exact decline date, proof of plan purchase, and serial number help clarify eligibility and date boundaries.
